content.text: What it is

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for a generative AI application is a machine-readable inventory of every component in the system: Python packages, OS-level libraries in the container base image, the LLM model weights (identified by name and cryptographic hash), prompt template files (by git hash), and any external tool dependencies the agent calls. SBOM generation is the automated process of producing this inventory as part of the CI/CD pipeline on every build.

SBOM generation for GenAI applications differs from conventional software SBOM generation because the dependency graph is broader. Beyond package dependencies, it must capture the identity of the model weights used for inference (since model weights are a first-class component that can have known vulnerabilities or supply-chain attacks), the version of prompt templates (which behave like code), and the identities of external APIs the agent calls at runtime.
Why it matters

US Executive Order 14028 on Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity mandated SBOMs for software sold to the federal government. The FDA requires SBOMs for software-as-a-medical-device. NTIA defined minimum SBOM elements. Beyond compliance, SBOMs provide the foundation for vulnerability management: when a new CVE is published for a package, you can immediately query all production systems to determine which ones include that package version, rather than scrambling to enumerate dependencies under incident pressure.

For GenAI applications specifically, SBOMs also document the model provenance chain. An SBOM that records the model name, version, and checksum provides evidence that the deployed model is the intended one and has not been tampered with between training and deployment. This matters for both security and regulatory accountability.
Benefits
Rapid CVE response.
When a new vulnerability is published (e.g., in a common HTTP library), querying SBOMs for all production services identifies the affected systems in seconds, enabling prioritized patching.
Regulatory compliance.
SBOMs satisfy EO 14028, FDA, and NTIA requirements for software transparency. They are also required by some enterprise procurement contracts.
Model provenance verification.
Recording the model weight checksum in the SBOM enables verification that the model deployed in production matches the one that was evaluated and approved.
Supply chain attack detection.
A signed SBOM attached to the container image allows downstream systems to verify that the image they pull matches the expected component set, detecting tampering.
License compliance.
SBOM scanners identify dependencies with license terms that conflict with the product's distribution model (e.g., GPL-licensed packages in a proprietary product).
Trade-offs and limitations
Model weights are not standard packages.
Standard SBOM tools (Syft, CycloneDX CLI) enumerate Python packages and OS libraries but do not natively enumerate model weights. Custom components must be added manually or via a wrapper script.
SBOM quality depends on lock files.

If a project does not use a lock file (poetry.lock, requirements.txt with pinned versions), the SBOM will show unpinned version ranges rather than exact versions, reducing its value for vulnerability matching.
Storage and distribution overhead.
Attaching a signed SBOM to each container image increases registry storage and image pull time slightly. For large deployments with frequent builds, SBOM artifact storage costs accumulate.
False positives in vulnerability matching.

Vulnerability scanners match SBOM components against CVE databases by package name and version. Some CVEs do not affect the code paths used by the application; teams must triage matches rather than treating every hit as a critical finding.
How it works

SBOM generation runs as a CI step after the container image is built. A tool such as Syft scans the image layers, enumerating all installed OS packages (from dpkg or rpm databases) and language-level packages (from Python's site-packages, Node's node_modules, etc.). The output is a structured document in SPDX or CycloneDX JSON format, listing each component with its name, version, package manager, and (where available) cryptographic checksums.

For GenAI-specific components, a wrapper script adds custom entries: the LLM model name and weight checksum (if weights are bundled in the image or fetched at startup), the version hashes of prompt template files checked into the repository, and the identifiers of external model APIs the agent calls. These custom components follow the CycloneDX component schema and are appended to the standard SBOM output.

The SBOM is then signed using Cosign and attached to the container image in the OCI registry as an attestation. A vulnerability scan (using Grype) is run against the SBOM as a CI gate: findings above a configurable severity threshold (typically CVSS 7.0 or higher for Critical and High) block the build. The SBOM artifact is retained as evidence for compliance and incident response.
Architecture

attrs.alt: SBOM generation for GenAI dependencies: four source inputs (Python deps, container layers, LLM weights reference, and prompt templates) flow into an SBOM Generator (Syft/CycloneDX). Outputs: signed SBOM document in SPDX/CycloneDX format, vulnerability scanner (Grype matches SBOM against CVE databases), and OCI registry attachment as a signed attestation. Compliance evidence feeds EO 14028, FDA, and NTIA minimum elements.

content.text: The generator runs in CI after image build. LLM weights and prompt template references are custom components added by a wrapper script. The signed SBOM is attached to the image in the registry and fed into the vulnerability gate.
When to use it
For any production system.
The cost of SBOM generation is low (a few seconds in CI); the value for CVE response and compliance is high. Generate SBOMs for all production services.
When selling to government or regulated industries.
SBOMs are mandatory for federal software procurement and are increasingly required in enterprise RFPs for healthcare, finance, and defense.
When model weights are bundled or fetched from a non-standard registry.
The SBOM provides the checksum evidence needed to verify that the weights in production match the approved version.
How to implement
Pin all package versions.
Use a lock file (poetry.lock, pip-compile output) and pin base image versions. Unpinned dependencies produce SBOMs with version ranges, not exact versions.
Add Syft to the CI pipeline after image build.
Run syft packages <image> -o cyclonedx-json to generate the SBOM. Store the output as a CI artifact.
Append GenAI custom components.
Write a script that reads the model name and weight checksum from the build environment and appends a custom CycloneDX component entry to the SBOM JSON.
Sign the SBOM with Cosign.
Run cosign attest --predicate <sbom.json> to sign and attach the SBOM to the image in the registry. This enables downstream verification.
Run Grype as a vulnerability gate.
Run grype sbom:<sbom.json> --fail-on high to block builds on high or critical findings. Manage false positives with a suppression list.
Store SBOMs with artifact retention policy.
Keep SBOMs for the lifetime of the corresponding deployment plus a retention buffer (at least 3 years for regulated industries).
Examples
GitHub Actions: generate and sign SBOM

name: Build and Generate SBOM

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build container image
        run: docker build -t agent-service:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Generate SBOM with Syft
        uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          image: agent-service:${{ github.sha }}
          format: cyclonedx-json
          output-file: sbom.json

      - name: Add GenAI custom components
        run: python3 scripts/add_genai_components.py sbom.json
        env:
          MODEL_NAME: claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219
          MODEL_SHA256: ${{ secrets.MODEL_WEIGHT_SHA256 }}
          PROMPT_GIT_HASH: ${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Scan SBOM for vulnerabilities
        uses: anchore/scan-action@v3
        with:
          sbom: sbom.json
          fail-build: true
          severity-cutoff: high

      - name: Sign and attach SBOM with Cosign
        run: |
          cosign attest \
            --predicate sbom.json \
            --type https://cyclonedx.org/bom \
            agent-service:${{ github.sha }}
        env:
          COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "1"  # use Sigstore keyless signing
Custom GenAI component appender

"""add_genai_components.py

Appends GenAI-specific components to a CycloneDX SBOM JSON.
Model weights and prompt template files are not enumerated by standard tools.
"""
import json
import os
import sys
from datetime import datetime, timezone


def make_model_component(model_name: str, sha256: str) -> dict:
    return {
        "type": "library",
        "name": model_name,
        "version": model_name,  # model name includes version for LLM APIs
        "purl": f"pkg:generic/{model_name}@{model_name}",
        "hashes": [{"alg": "SHA-256", "content": sha256}],
        "description": "LLM model weights reference",
        "properties": [{"name": "genai:type", "value": "model-weights"}],
    }


def make_prompt_component(git_hash: str) -> dict:
    return {
        "type": "library",
        "name": "prompt-templates",
        "version": git_hash,
        "description": "Prompt template files at git commit",
        "properties": [
            {"name": "genai:type", "value": "prompt-templates"},
            {"name": "genai:git-hash", "value": git_hash},
        ],
    }


def main():
    sbom_path = sys.argv[1]
    with open(sbom_path) as f:
        sbom = json.load(f)

    components = sbom.get("components", [])
    components.append(make_model_component(
        model_name=os.environ["MODEL_NAME"],
        sha256=os.environ["MODEL_SHA256"],
    ))
    components.append(make_prompt_component(
        git_hash=os.environ["PROMPT_GIT_HASH"],
    ))
    sbom["components"] = components
    sbom["metadata"]["timestamp"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

    with open(sbom_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(sbom, f, indent=2)
    print(f"Added GenAI components to {sbom_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Real-world example

In 2024, a critical vulnerability in a popular Python HTTP library (used by virtually all agents that call external APIs) was disclosed. Teams with automated SBOM generation queried their artifact registry within minutes to identify which services included the vulnerable version. Teams without SBOMs spent days manually auditing requirements files across dozens of services, some of which had outdated documentation.

An AI platform team that had added model weight checksums to their SBOMs was able to quickly verify that their production deployments were using the expected model version when a supply-chain attack against a model hosting provider was reported. Their SBOM proved their deployed checksum did not match the tampered version distributed by the attacker.
Common mistakes
Not pinning package versions.
An SBOM generated from unpinned requirements lists version ranges, not exact versions. Vulnerability scanners cannot match ranges reliably.
Omitting model weights and prompt templates.
A GenAI SBOM that only enumerates Python packages misses the two most GenAI-specific components: the model and the prompts.
Generating the SBOM from source, not the built image.
Scanning the source directory misses OS-level packages installed in the container. Scan the built image to capture all layers.
No vulnerability gate.
Generating an SBOM without scanning it for known vulnerabilities produces a compliance artifact that does not improve security posture.
Not storing SBOMs long-term.
Future CVEs may affect components in today's release. Retaining SBOMs for the life of the deployment enables retrospective impact assessment.
Best practices
Generate the SBOM from the built container image, not from source files, to capture all OS-layer dependencies.
Add GenAI-specific custom components for model weights (name and SHA256) and prompt templates (git hash).
Sign the SBOM with Cosign and attach it to the OCI image as an attestation for downstream verification.
Run a vulnerability gate (Grype, Trivy) against the SBOM in CI and block on high/critical findings.
Retain SBOMs for the lifetime of the deployment plus at least 3 years.
Pin all package versions using a lock file to ensure SBOMs contain exact versions, not ranges.
Build a CVE-to-service query capability so any new CVE can be assessed for production impact in seconds.
Performance and scalability

SBOM generation adds 15-60 seconds to a typical CI build (depending on image size and number of packages). Signing with Cosign adds another 5-10 seconds. At high build volumes, these steps can be parallelized with the container push step. Store SBOMs in the OCI registry as attestations rather than in a separate database to keep distribution and storage simple; registry storage costs for SBOMs are negligible.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Syft (Anchore)
SBOM generation
Scans container images and filesystems; outputs SPDX or CycloneDX JSON
Grype (Anchore)
Vulnerability scanning
Matches SBOM against CVE databases; integrates with Syft output
Cosign (Sigstore)
SBOM signing
Keyless or key-based signing; attaches SBOM to OCI image
Trivy (Aqua Security)
Combined scanner
SBOM generation and vulnerability scanning in one tool; supports SPDX and CycloneDX
CycloneDX tools
SBOM standard
CycloneDX JSON schema with custom components for GenAI; strong tooling ecosystem

Security and reliability

SBOM signing with Cosign (or GPG) ensures that any modification to the SBOM after generation is detectable. The signature is attached to the OCI image in the registry and can be verified at deploy time using cosign verify-attestation. For regulated environments, combine SBOM signing with SLSA provenance attestation to provide a complete chain of custody from source code to deployed artifact.

For reliability, the SBOM generation step should not block deployments for transient tool failures. Configure the CI pipeline to allow the SBOM step to retry twice before failing. The vulnerability gate should remain blocking; a failed SBOM generation should produce a blocking alert but allow the team to investigate rather than hard-blocking a critical fix.
Key takeaways
SBOM generation for GenAI applications must include model weight references and prompt template versions, not just Python packages and OS libraries.
Generate SBOMs from the built container image, not from source, to capture all OS-layer dependencies added by the Dockerfile.
Sign and attach SBOMs to OCI images as attestations using Cosign; unsigned SBOMs provide no tamper protection.
Run a vulnerability gate against the SBOM in CI; generating an SBOM without scanning it only produces compliance paperwork without security value.
Pin all package versions using lock files; SBOMs with version ranges cannot be reliably matched against CVE databases.
Retain SBOMs for the lifetime of the deployment to enable retrospective CVE impact assessment when new vulnerabilities are disclosed against old package versions.
Build a CVE-to-service query so new vulnerabilities can be assessed for production impact in seconds rather than through manual audits.
